pick.rationale: Purdue enters as one of the nation's elite programs ranked 8th in both Torvik and KenPom systems with a dominant 26-8 Big Ten record, while Queens is a mid-major champion making their first-ever NCAA tournament appearance. The efficiency margin gap is staggering at +30.5 for Purdue versus -1.45 for Queens, reflecting elite offensive production (ranked 1st nationally in Torvik at 132.56 adjO) combined with solid defensive efficiency (100.23 adjD). In a classic 2/15 seed matchup where two seeds historically win approximately 85-90% of the time, Purdue's superior talent depth, system consistency, and massive performance disparity across every metric create an insurmountable advantage over the inexperienced Royals.

reasoningStep.title: Purdue vs Queens - Advanced Metrics Comparison

reasoningStep.evidence: Purdue: Torvik rank 8, KenPom rank 8, AdjEM +30.5, AdjO 132.56 (1st nationally), AdjD 100.23 (36th), Record 26-8 B10. Queens: Torvik rank 199, KenPom rank 181, AdjEM -1.45, AdjO 114.57 (85th), AdjD 117.06 (316th), Record 21-13 ASUN, First NCAA Tournament appearance. Historical 2/15 seed success rate: 85-90%. No significant injury concerns identified for Purdue.

(1:29) Myron Medcalf Mar 15, 2026, 06:45 PM ET Close Covers college basketball Joined ESPN.com in 2011 Graduate of Minnesota State University, Mankato Follow on X Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions Get your popcorn ready because this NCAA tournament could be one of the best in recent memory. The top seeds are elite programs with the potential to cut down the nets. The underdogs are dangerous, too. The field also has undeniable star power. Top transfers such as Yaxel Lendeborg at Michigan and freshman stars (see: AJ Dybantsa , a candidate for the No. 1 pick in the 2026 NBA draft) are all in the same tier of teams this year, chasing the same dreams. It's a lot to consider. But we're here to help. Our annual NCAA tournament guide gives you the information you need to know about each team, pertinent information about any current issues that could impact their postseason, key injuries to note -- and, finally, our projections on their ceiling. We're got enough to help the nerds and the newcomers to the NCAA tournament chaos make sense of everything. Here is what you need to know about all 68 teams in the NCAA tournament. modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.
Bracket config id: demo-2026-bracket
Tournament year: 2026
Current game id: r64-west-8
Current game label: West 2/15
Round: Round of 64
Resolved teams: Purdue (purdue) vs Queens (queens)
Prior committed picks: 19

I need to predict the winner of game r64-west-8, which is Purdue (2-seed) vs Queens (15-seed). This is a classic 2/15 matchup.

Let me gather relevant information about both teams:
1. First, I should look up their CBB ratings to compare advanced metrics
2. Then search for any injury news or recent team context
3. Consider historical 2/15 seed matchups in the tournament

Let me start by looking up the ratings for both teams.

Great! Now I have the ratings. Let me analyze:

Purdue (2-seed):
- Torvik rank: 8th, KenPom rank: 8th
- Record: 26-8
- AdjO: 132.56 (1st nationally in Torvik), 131 (2nd in KenPom)
- AdjD: 100.23 (36th in Torvik), 100.5 (KenPom)
- AdjEM: +30.5 (elite efficiency margin)

Queens (15-seed):
- Torvik rank: 199th, KenPom rank: 181st
- Record: 21-13
- AdjO: 114.57 (85th in Torvik), 115.8 (78th in KenPom)
- AdjD: 117.06 (316th in Torvik - very poor), 117.3 (poor in KenPom)
- AdjEM: -1.45 (slightly negative)

The gap is massive:
- Efficiency margin: Purdue +30.5 vs Queens -1.45 = ~32 point difference
- Offensive rating: Purdue 132.56 vs Queens 114.57 = ~18 point difference
- Defensive rating: Purdue 100.23 vs Queens 117.06 = ~17 point difference

This is a classic 2/15 matchup where 2-seeds historically win 85-90% of the time. The advanced metrics show Purdue as one of the elite teams in the country (top 10 in both systems) while Queens is a mid-major team with mediocre metrics.

Let me search for any injury news or recent context that might affect this game.

modelTrace.content:
I don't have specific injury information, but based on the data I've gathered:

Purdue (2-seed):
- Elite team ranked 8th in both Torvik and KenPom
- #1 offensive rating in Torvik (132.56), #2 in KenPom (131)
- Strong defensive rating (100.23/100.5)
- Massive +30.5 efficiency margin
- 26-8 record in Big Ten
- No reported injuries affecting key players

Queens (15-seed):
- Mid-major team ranked 199th/181st
- Mediocre offense (114.57/115.8 - 85th/78th nationally)
- Poor defense (117.06/117.3 - 316th/very poor)
- Slightly negative efficiency margin (-1.45)
- 21-13 record in ASUN
- First-ever NCAA tournament appearance

This is a classic 2/15 matchup where 2-seeds win approximately 85-90% of the time historically. The advanced metrics show an enormous gap - Purdue's +30.5 efficiency margin vs Queens' -1.45 is a ~32 point difference. Purdue has one of the best offenses in the country while Queens has one of the worst defenses.

There's no indication of significant injuries to Purdue that would change this assessment. Queens is making their first tournament appearance with no experience at this level.

The pick should be Purdue with high confidence.
